questions.question: The constitutional guarantee of fair procedures in Ireland is primarily grounded in:

options.A: Art. 34.1 — administration of justice in courts
options.B: Art. 38.1 (in criminal proceedings) and Art. 40.3 (in all proceedings affecting personal rights) — requiring that decisions affecting persons be taken fairly and in accordance with natural justice
options.C: Art. 15.2 — the legislative power of the Oireachtas
options.D: Art. 6 ECHR only — there is no indigenous constitutional basis for fair procedures

questions.explanation: Fair procedures in criminal proceedings derive from Art. 38.1's 'due course of law' guarantee. More broadly, the constitutional guarantee of fair procedures — encompassing natural justice — is also grounded in Art. 40.3, which obligates the State to defend and vindicate personal rights. The Supreme Court in cases such as East Donegal Co-operative v AG [1970] IR 317 and In re Haughey [1971] IR 217 confirmed that constitutional fair procedures apply to any tribunal or body that makes decisions affecting a person's rights or legitimate expectations.

questions.question: The two principal rules of natural justice recognised in Irish constitutional law are:

options.A: Nemo iudex in causa sua (no person should be a judge in their own cause — the rule against bias) and audi alteram partem (hear the other side — the right to be heard)
options.B: Nemo dat quod non habet (no one can give what they do not have) and in dubio pro reo (in doubt, for the accused)
options.C: Actus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea and nullum crimen sine lege
options.D: Ubi ius ibi remedium (where there is a right there is a remedy) and ex turpi causa non oritur actio

questions.explanation: The two foundational rules of natural justice in Irish law are: (1) Nemo iudex in causa sua — no person should adjudicate in a matter in which they have a personal interest or bias; and (2) Audi alteram partem — no decision should be made affecting a person's rights without giving them an adequate opportunity to be heard. These rules, long established in common law, have constitutional status in Ireland and apply to courts, tribunals, regulatory bodies, and any decision-maker whose determinations affect constitutionally protected rights.

questions.question: In re Haughey [1971] IR 217 (Supreme Court) is a landmark authority on constitutional fair procedures. It established that a person whose 'good name' (Art. 40.3.2°) is at stake before a committee or tribunal is entitled to:

options.A: Trial by jury as of right before any finding adverse to their reputation
options.B: A copy of the evidence against them, the right to cross-examine witnesses, the right to give rebutting evidence, and the right to legal representation — minimum constitutional procedural guarantees
options.C: An automatic right of appeal to the Supreme Court from any adverse finding
options.D: Full publication of all proceedings to ensure public accountability

questions.explanation: In re Haughey [1971] IR 217, the Supreme Court held that where proceedings before a committee of the Oireachtas could adversely affect a person's constitutional right to a good name (Art. 40.3.2°), the affected person was entitled to the following minimum constitutional procedural guarantees: (i) a copy of the evidence bearing on their reputation; (ii) the right to cross-examine witnesses whose evidence affects them adversely; (iii) the right to give rebutting evidence; and (iv) the right to address the committee (including through counsel). These 'Haughey rights' represent the constitutional minimum of fair procedure in adversarial proceedings.

questions.question: The constitutional test for apparent bias in Irish law is:

options.A: Whether the decision-maker had an actual intention to decide unfairly
options.B: Whether a reasonable and informed observer, with knowledge of all relevant facts, would apprehend a real possibility or real danger that the decision-maker would not bring an impartial mind to bear on the determination
options.C: Whether the decision-maker was personally acquainted with any party to the proceedings
options.D: Whether the decision-maker had previously expressed any view on any aspect of the subject matter

questions.explanation: The Irish test for disqualifying bias was definitively stated in O'Callaghan v Mahon [2007] IESC 17 (Mahon Tribunal case) and is the 'reasonable apprehension of bias' test: the question is whether a reasonable person, fully informed of all relevant circumstances, would apprehend a real possibility that the decision-maker would not bring an impartial mind to bear. This test does not require proof of actual bias — apprehended or apparent bias suffices. It is objective, assessed from the perspective of the informed observer, not the subjective perception of the affected party.

questions.question: The right to be heard (audi alteram partem) requires, at minimum, that a person whose rights are affected by a decision:

options.A: Must have access to a full oral hearing before a judge in every case
options.B: Must be given adequate notice of the case against them and a fair opportunity to respond, the precise form of which depends on the context and the nature of the rights at stake
options.C: Must be permitted to call any witnesses they wish at any stage of any proceedings
options.D: Must be given a written decision with reasons in every case without exception

questions.explanation: The content of the audi alteram partem requirement is flexible and context-dependent. The Supreme Court has consistently held that the procedural requirements necessary to satisfy constitutional fair procedures vary with the nature and gravity of the decision and the rights at stake. A decision affecting minor administrative interests may require no more than an opportunity to make written submissions; one affecting liberty or a person's good name may require a full oral hearing, cross-examination rights, and legal representation. The core requirement is adequate notice of the case to be met and a fair opportunity to respond.

questions.question: East Donegal Co-operative Livestock Mart Ltd v Attorney General [1970] IR 317 (Supreme Court) is important for which proposition in relation to fair procedures?

options.A: That the right to fair procedures applies only to courts and not to administrative bodies
options.B: That post-1937 legislation is to be presumed constitutional and interpreted in conformity with the Constitution, including its implied procedural requirements — and that the courts will read implied fair procedure requirements into legislation even where not expressly stated
options.C: That the Oireachtas can exclude fair procedures by express statutory provision
options.D: That natural justice applies only in criminal proceedings, not in civil or administrative matters

questions.explanation: East Donegal Co-operative v AG [1970] IR 317 is foundational for both the presumption of constitutionality and for the principle that legislation is presumed to require its powers to be exercised in accordance with constitutional fair procedures. Walsh J held that even where a statute grants wide administrative discretion without expressly mandating procedural safeguards, the courts will imply that those powers must be exercised in conformity with constitutional requirements of fair procedures and natural justice. This principle significantly expanded the reach of constitutional fair procedures into administrative decision-making.

questions.question: A planning authority refuses planning permission to a landowner without informing the landowner of the reason for refusal and without giving them an opportunity to address concerns about the application. This most likely violates:

options.A: No constitutional provision — planning matters are entirely within the discretion of the planning authority
options.B: The constitutional guarantee of fair procedures (Art. 40.3 and the audi alteram partem rule) — an affected landowner must be given an adequate opportunity to be heard before an adverse decision affecting their property rights is made
options.C: Art. 43 — private property rights can never be restricted by planning decisions
options.D: Art. 34.1 — all decisions affecting property must be taken by courts, not administrative bodies

questions.explanation: A decision by a planning authority to refuse planning permission affects a landowner's constitutional property rights (Art. 43, Art. 40.3.2°). Constitutional fair procedures require that the affected person receive adequate notice of any material concerns and a fair opportunity to address them before the decision is made. Failure to give reasons or to allow the applicant to respond to concerns that form the basis of the refusal violates the audi alteram partem rule as constitutionally understood. Planning legislation (e.g., the Planning and Development Act 2000) incorporates these requirements, as is constitutionally required under East Donegal.

questions.question: A tribunal of inquiry is investigating allegations of corruption against a named individual. The individual argues they have a constitutional right to cross-examine witnesses who give evidence adverse to them. In light of In re Haughey [1971] IR 217, this claim is:

options.A: Unfounded — tribunals of inquiry are investigative, not adjudicative, so natural justice does not apply
options.B: Well-founded — where a tribunal's findings could adversely affect a person's constitutional right to a good name, that person has a constitutional right to cross-examine adverse witnesses as part of the minimum procedural guarantees established in In re Haughey
options.C: Partially founded — the individual may only cross-examine through written questions, not orally through counsel
options.D: Dependent on whether the relevant Tribunal Act expressly provides for cross-examination

questions.explanation: In re Haughey [1971] IR 217 established that where proceedings can damage a person's constitutionally protected good name (Art. 40.3.2°), the affected person has a constitutional right to cross-examine witnesses whose evidence bears adversely on their reputation. This principle applies to tribunals of inquiry — a constitutional context extensively litigated in the context of Irish public inquiries (Tribunal of Inquiry Acts). The right exists as a constitutional minimum regardless of what the enabling legislation provides; if the legislation fails to provide for it, the court will imply it or declare the procedure unconstitutional.

questions.question: The Supreme Court in O'Brien v Bord na Móna [1983] IR 255 held that an employee dismissed without fair procedures had:

options.A: No constitutional remedy — employment disputes are purely a matter of contract law
options.B: A constitutional right to fair procedures before dismissal, derived from Art. 40.3 — the courts would intervene where fundamental rights were violated even in the employment context
options.C: An automatic right to reinstatement under the Constitution
options.D: Rights governed exclusively by the Unfair Dismissals Act 1977, which displaced the constitutional claim

questions.explanation: O'Brien v Bord na Móna [1983] IR 255 illustrates the constitutional dimension of employment fair procedures. The Supreme Court recognised that where a dismissal by a body exercises public functions or where the employment is protected by constitutional rights, Art. 40.3 requires that fair procedures be observed before dismissal. The reach of constitutional fair procedures into employment has been developed through cases involving the Civil Service, statutory bodies, and other entities exercising public power. The extent to which constitutional fair procedures overlay private employment (as opposed to public employment) remains a nuanced area.

questions.question: The constitutional requirement of reasons for administrative decisions — where the decision affects constitutionally protected rights — is best explained as:

options.A: An absolute rule requiring written reasons for every administrative decision without exception
options.B: A constitutional requirement that persons whose rights are adversely affected by decisions be given sufficient reasons to understand why the decision was made and to exercise their right of appeal or judicial review meaningfully, though the content and form of reasons required varies with context
options.C: A purely statutory requirement — the Constitution does not require reasons for administrative decisions
options.D: A requirement applying only to decisions of constitutional courts, not administrative tribunals

questions.explanation: Irish courts have consistently held that where a decision affects a person's constitutional rights, fairness requires that the affected person be given adequate reasons to understand the basis of the decision. This is necessary to enable effective exercise of any right of appeal or judicial review. The degree of detail required varies with context — the Constitution does not require exhaustive written reasons in every case, but where the decision is adverse and seriously affects rights, inadequate or no reasons may constitute a violation of constitutional fair procedures. This principle is grounded in Art. 40.3 and the overall constitutional requirement of procedural fairness.
